title: St. James the Apostle Son of Zebedee, St. Simeon of Persia & St. Adrian of Corinth
narrative:
St. James the Apostle Son of Zebedee, St. Simeon of Persia the Martyr-Bishop, and St. Adrian of Corinth

On the seventeenth of Miazia, the holy Church enters the illuminated second half with the Apostle James, the first of the Twelve to drink the cup that Christ had foretold — and his presence here, in the dawning half of the month, shifts the martyrological register from the expectant to the victorious. James was beheaded by Herod Agrippa to please the Jerusalem crowd, and the Acts of the Apostles records the event in two verses so brief that the original Greek can be read in thirty seconds, as though Luke could not bear to dwell on it. The tradition, by contrast, has dwelt on it for centuries, and the detail it has most treasured is the story of the officer who led James to his execution: that during the proceedings this man, moved by James's testimony, declared himself a Christian, asked James to forgive him for his role in his arrest, and was executed alongside him. The two arrived at the place of execution as prisoner and guard; they left it as brothers in martyrdom. The illumination of Miazia's second half begins in precisely this way: the victory of grace over institutional violence, the transforming of the executioner by the one being executed.

Saint Simeon of Persia — the great bishop-martyr of the Persian church also known as Simeon bar Sabbae, the Archbishop of Seleucia-Ctesiphon — is one of the most significant figures in the history of the Oriental churches and one of the most moving stories in the entire Synaxarium tradition. Arrested by Shapur II along with members of his clergy on Good Friday of the year three hundred and forty-one, Simeon refused to purchase his freedom by a payment of tribute to the Persian throne — arguing that a shepherd could not save himself at the expense of his flock — and was executed on Holy Saturday alongside over one hundred of his clergy. The timing is the tradition's most concentrated theological statement: the archbishop of the Persian church was martyred between Good Friday and the Resurrection, making his death the most literal possible participation in the mystery of Holy Saturday, the mystery of the Church lying in the tomb between the death of Christ and the proclamation of the empty tomb.

Saint Adrian of Corinth is this day's discovery: a Roman soldier stationed at Corinth who was converted to Christianity through witnessing the martyrdom of one of the Corinthian confessors and who immediately presented himself before his commanding officer to declare his faith. His conversion is the Corinthian version of the pattern that the Miazia second half has already established: the official of the system being converted by the witness of the system's victims, the illumination spreading from the martyrs outward into the community of those who thought themselves observers. Adrian was executed on the same day he converted, his martyrdom arriving before his baptism, which the tradition received as blood-baptism and which it honours with the same dignity as sacramental baptism. Amen.
